Hinglish tip: 'AUTHORITY-CHECK ke baad SY-SUBRC check karna bhool jaana' — yeh specific gotcha mention karna interviewer ko impress karta hai. Ye dikhata hai ki tum sirf theory nahi jaante, real coding mistakes bhi jaante ho. Authorization object SE11 mein define karna bhi bonus point hai.
enhancements-027.codeExamples:
" Authorization issue example in BAdI
" BAD: Missing SY-SUBRC check after AUTHORITY-CHECK
METHOD if_ex_badi_vendor_price~read_price.
  " Authority check present but SY-SUBRC not validated!
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'M_LFB1_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'. " 03 = display
  " BUG: Missing: IF sy-subrc <> 0. MESSAGE ... RETURN. ENDIF.

  " This SELECT runs even if authority check failed!
  SELECT ekpo~netpr FROM ekpo
    WHERE lifnr = @lv_vendor INTO @lv_price.
ENDMETHOD.

" GOOD: Complete authorization implementation
METHOD if_ex_badi_vendor_price~read_price.
  " Custom authorization object for vendor pricing
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZVEND_PRICE'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.  " CRITICAL: Always check SY-SUBRC
    MESSAGE 'No authorization to view vendor pricing' TYPE 'E'.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  " Only authorized users reach here
  SELECT SINGLE netpr FROM ekpo
    WHERE lifnr = @lv_vendor
    INTO @lv_price.
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you define a custom authorization object for an enhancement?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SE11 transaction mein Authorization Object maintain karo. Create → Object name (ZVENDOR_PRICE format) → Description → Authorization Class assign karo (jaise SD ya MM — relevant area) → Fields add karo (jaise BUKRS company code, ACTVT activity 01/02/03/06). Save karo. Phir SUIM ya PFCG mein roles mein yeh object assign karo with specific field values. Enhancement code mein: AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZVENDOR_PRICE' ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'. SY-SUBRC check karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: To define a custom authorization object: open SE11, create a new Authorization Object — name starting with Z, for example ZVENDOR_PRICE — assign it to a relevant authorization object class such as SD or MM, and add authorization fields like BUKRS for company code and ACTVT for activity type values 01 create, 02 change, 03 display. Activate the object. Assign it to appropriate roles in PFCG with permitted field values. In the enhancement code, use AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZVENDOR_PRICE' ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03' and always check SY-SUBRC immediately after.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Custom authorization object is the professional solution when enhancement accesses data beyond standard transaction scope. It gives security team full control over who sees what — without changing enhancement code. Adding new user access = just update the role in PFCG, no code change needed.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Hard-coding user names or role names in enhancement code for access control — like checking SY-UNAME = 'ADMIN'. This is not maintainable and bypasses SAP authorization framework. Always use AUTHORITY-CHECK with proper authorization objects.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11: Create Authorization Object ZVENDOR_PRICE
" Object Class: SD
" Fields:
"   BUKRS (type BUKRS) - Company Code
"   ACTVT (type ACTVT_AUTH) - Activity: 03=Display, 06=Delete

" PFCG: Assign to role Z_MM_BUYER
"   ZVENDOR_PRICE: BUKRS = 1000, ACTVT = 03

" Enhancement code:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZVENDOR_PRICE'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_company_code
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.  " 03 = Display
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE e001(zauth) WITH 'Vendor pricing view not authorized'.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.
" Safe to access price data now

followupAnswerBank.question: What is SU53 and how do you use it to debug authorization failures in enhancements?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SU53 transaction last failed authority check dikhata hai — current session ya specific user ke liye. Jab user ko 'No authorization' message aata hai — user SU53 run kare ya tum SU53 mein username enter karke dekho. SU53 dikhata hai: kaun sa authorization object fail hua, kaun sa field, kaun sa value expected tha, user ke paas kya value thi. Agar tumhara enhancement AUTHORITY-CHECK fail karta hai, SU53 mein clearly dikh jaayega: 'Object ZVENDOR_PRICE, field BUKRS, required 1000, user has no value.' Yeh information PFCG mein role update karne ke liye kaafi hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: SU53 displays the last failed authorization check for the current user or for a specified user. When an enhancement triggers an AUTHORITY-CHECK that fails, the user sees an error message. You or the user can then run SU53 to see exactly which authorization object failed, which field values were checked, what values the user's role provides, and what was required. This information is used to update the user's role in PFCG to add the missing authorization. In enhancement debugging, SU53 is the first tool to use when users report authorization errors — it shows the exact object and field combination that needs to be granted.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SU53 saves hours of authorization debugging. Without it, you would have to read through the AUTHORITY-CHECK code, trace which object and fields are checked, then compare with user role assignments. SU53 shows the exact failure in one screen. Learn SU53 — it comes up in every authorization discussion.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not directing users to run SU53 when they report authorization errors. Some developers try to read all AUTHORITY-CHECK code manually to find the issue. SU53 shows you instantly. Also: SU53 only shows the LAST failed check — if user had multiple authorization issues, run the transaction step by step and check SU53 after each failure.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SU53 usage for enhancement authorization debug:
" 1. User runs MIGO, gets error: 'No authorization'
" 2. User runs SU53 (or you run SU53 -> Enter Username)
" 3. SU53 shows:
"    Object: ZVENDOR_PRICE
"    Field BUKRS: Required = 1000, User has = (blank)
"    Field ACTVT: Required = 03,   User has = (blank)
" 4. Go to PFCG -> find user's role -> add ZVENDOR_PRICE
"    with BUKRS = 1000, ACTVT = 03 -> regenerate -> transport
" 5. User retests -> authorized now

" In enhancement code — ensure AUTHORITY-CHECK is present:
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZVENDOR_PRICE'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Missing authorization' TYPE 'E'. " <- SU53 captures this
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: Can an enhancement accidentally grant more authorizations than intended?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Haan — privilege escalation. Example: standard transaction T-code X ko sirf plant managers execute kar sakte hain — they have AUTHORITY-CHECK for specific plants only. Agar enhancement us transaction ke andar koi system call ya FM call karta hai using a service user account ya AUTHORITY-CHECK bypass karta hai — regular users effectively woh capability gain kar lete hain without their own authorization. Another example: CALL FUNCTION ... DESTINATION 'RFCDEST' — RFC call karna enhancement mein — agar RFC destination sirf privileged users ke liye hai but enhancement har user ke liye RFC call karta hai, toh authorization boundary cross ho gayi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Yes, enhancements can inadvertently grant privilege escalation. Examples: an enhancement that calls a function module using a trusted RFC destination or a service user context may execute with higher privileges than the calling user has. An enhancement that reads data using ABAP authority bypass statements or calling privileged programs via SUBMIT or CALL TRANSACTION effectively gives all enhancement users the same access as the privileged context. Prevention: never use authority bypass in enhancement code, always perform AUTHORITY-CHECK with the calling user's context, avoid calling privileged RFC destinations from enhancements accessible to all users. Have a security architect review enhancements that involve RFC calls or cross-system access.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Privilege escalation through enhancements is a real security finding in SAP security audits. Auditors look for AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT '...' FIELD DUMMY bypass patterns, SELECT with sy-uname override, and RFC calls in enhancement code. Knowing these patterns shows security awareness.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using AUTHORITY-CHECK ... FIELD DUMMY as a quick fix to suppress authorization errors in enhancement code — this completely bypasses the check and grants access to everyone. Never use FIELD DUMMY except in very specific justified cases with security team approval.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" DANGEROUS pattern — authority bypass in enhancement
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'M_LFB1_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD 'DUMMY'.  " <- bypasses check! grants all!
" This means ALL users pass this check regardless of their roles

" SAFE pattern — proper check with actual field value
AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'M_LFB1_BUK'
  ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs  " actual company code checked
  ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'No authorization for company code' TYPE 'E'.
  RETURN. " <- hard stop, no bypass
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What authorization considerations apply to enhancements that post data to the database?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Data posting enhancements — jaise BAdI jo INSERT karta hai Z-table mein ya standard posting FM call karta hai — ke liye: ACTVT 01 (create) ya 02 (change) authorization check karo pehle. Example: BAdI goods receipt ke baad ek custom audit table mein INSERT karta hai — user ke paas woh table write access honi chahiye — AUTHORITY-CHECK karo pehle INSERT se. RFC posting ke liye — RFC destination authorization check karo. ABAP Update Task (CALL FUNCTION ... IN UPDATE TASK) ke liye — same user context mein update chalta hai, user ki authorizations apply hoti hain. Change document posting ke liye — standard authorization objects follow karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Enhancements that write or post data must check ACTVT 01 for create or ACTVT 02 for change before performing the write operation. If the enhancement inserts into a custom Z-table, define an authorization object for that table with BUKRS and ACTVT fields and check it before INSERT. For enhancements that call posting function modules or BAPIs, the called FM performs its own authorization checks — but the enhancement must not bypass those by calling with privileged context. Enhancements using CALL FUNCTION IN UPDATE TASK run in the same user session context, so user authorizations apply. Document all write operations in enhancement code during security review so the authorization concept covers them.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security audit finding: enhancement writes to a custom table without authorization check — any user who can run the transaction can write to that table regardless of their intended access level. This is a critical finding. Always add authorization check before any INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE in enhancement code.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming that if the standard transaction has authorization checks, the enhancement's additional writes are also covered — they are not. Enhancement code's own write operations need their own authorization checks. Standard transaction checks cover standard program flow only.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Authorization check before write in enhancement
METHOD if_ex_mb_migo_badi~post_document.
  " Check authorization before INSERT to custom audit table
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZAUDIT_LOG'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD lv_bukrs
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '01'.  " 01 = Create
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    " Log the failed attempt but do not stop GR posting
    " (audit logging failure is not a business process block)
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  " Authorized: proceed with INSERT
  INSERT INTO zaudit_gr_log VALUES @ls_audit_record.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    MESSAGE 'Audit log insert failed' TYPE 'W'.
  ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle authorization in a filter-dependent BAdI with multiple implementations?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Filter-dependent BAdI mein multiple implementations hain — company code 1000 ke liye ek, 2000 ke liye ek. Authorization challenge: kya har implementation mein alag authorization check hona chahiye? Haan — agar implementations alag sensitive data access karti hain. Implementation for company code 1000 shayad 1000 ke vendor data access kare — AUTHORITY-CHECK with BUKRS = 1000 karo. Implementation for company code 2000 same check karo BUKRS = 2000 ke liye. Filter itself authorization provide nahi karta — sirf routing karta hai. Authorization check implementation ke andar karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Filter-dependent BAdI implementations each run for their specific filter context — for example company code 1000 triggers implementation A and company code 2000 triggers implementation B. Each implementation must independently perform authorization checks relevant to its data access scope. The BAdI filter mechanism routes execution but does not grant authorization — a user assigned to company code 1000 filter context could still access 2000 data if implementation A reads cross-company data without checking. Each implementation should check authority for its specific company code and data scope independently. Do not assume filter assignment implies authorization.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: This is an advanced authorization question that shows you understand the distinction between routing (what filter does) and authorization (what AUTHORITY-CHECK does). Interviewers asking about filter BAdIs and authorization want to see this distinction explicitly stated.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Thinking BAdI filter = authorization boundary. It is not — filter controls which implementation runs, but the implementation must still perform its own authorization checks for the data it accesses.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Each filter-dependent implementation checks its own auth
" Implementation A: for BUKRS = 1000
METHOD if_ex_zfin_badi~process.
  " Do NOT assume filter = authorized
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD '1000'  " explicit check for 1000
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RETURN. " Not authorized even though filter matched
  ENDIF.
  " Access company 1000 specific financial data
ENDMETHOD.

" Implementation B: for BUKRS = 2000 — same pattern
METHOD if_ex_zfin_badi~process.
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'F_BKPF_BUK'
    ID 'BUKRS' FIELD '2000'  " separate explicit check
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '03'.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0. RETURN. ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the risk of using SUBMIT or CALL TRANSACTION inside an enhancement?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SUBMIT program ya CALL TRANSACTION kisi doosre program ko call karta hai. Security risk: agar tum SUBMIT RPROC000 (payroll program) ya CALL TRANSACTION 'PA30' (HR master data change) enhancement mein karo — user effectively woh action kar sakta hai chahe unke paas directly woh T-code ka authorization na ho. Enhancement ne unhe bypass path de diya. CALL TRANSACTION has authorization check usually — lekin SUBMIT background mein call kar sakta hai bina user dialog authorization check ke agar AND SKIP FIRST SCREEN options use kiye. Always security review karo before using SUBMIT or CALL TRANSACTION in enhancement.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Using SUBMIT or CALL TRANSACTION inside an enhancement is a significant security risk because it allows the enhancement user to trigger programs or transactions they may not be directly authorized for. CALL TRANSACTION normally performs transaction authorization checks, but SUBMIT with additional parameters can bypass interactive authorization screens. If an enhancement accessible to all warehouse users calls SUBMIT to a payroll or HR program, those users effectively gain access to payroll processing they should not have. Security rule: do not use SUBMIT or CALL TRANSACTION in enhancements without explicit security review and justification. Use BAPI calls or function modules with explicit AUTHORITY-CHECK instead.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SAP security auditors specifically look for SUBMIT and CALL TRANSACTION statements in enhancement code — they are flagged in automated security scans. If found, you must justify why they are needed and prove authorization is not bypassed. Avoiding them entirely is simpler and safer.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using SUBMIT ... AND RETURN in enhancement code for convenience without security review. This is a shortcut that creates audit findings. Use proper BAPI or FM calls with explicit authorization checks instead.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" RISKY: SUBMIT inside enhancement
" This could give warehouse user payroll program access!
METHOD if_ex_mb_migo_badi~post_document.
  SUBMIT rproc000  " payroll program!
    WITH... AND RETURN.  " <- bypass risk!
ENDMETHOD.

" SAFER: Use BAPI with proper authorization
METHOD if_ex_mb_migo_badi~post_document.
  " Explicit authorization check first
  AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZPAYROLL_ACC'
    ID 'ACTVT' FIELD '01'.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0. RETURN. ENDIF.
  " Use BAPI — it performs its own checks internally
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE'
    EXPORTING... " proper parameter passing
  " BAPI does standard auth checks — safer than SUBMIT
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document authorization requirements for an enhancement during development?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Enhancement development mein authorization documentation ek security concept document mein honi chahiye. Document mein: enhancement ka naam aur transaction, data access list (kaunse tables read/write hote hain), standard authorization objects used (jo enhancement internally check karta hai), custom authorization objects defined (ZXXX objects jo tumne banaye), roles impacted (kaunsi roles mein yeh objects assign karne hain), security review sign-off. Yeh document Basis/Security team ko QA transport ke pehle share karo — taaki woh roles update kar saken pehle go-live se.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Authorization documentation for an enhancement should cover: the enhancement name and triggered transaction, complete list of data accessed — tables read and written — with sensitivity classification, all standard authorization objects checked in the enhancement code, any custom authorization objects defined with their field specifications, roles that must be updated to include new authorization objects and their permitted values, and a security team review sign-off before production transport. This documentation supports the Basis security team in role updates and forms part of the go-live security checklist. For regulated industries, this document is mandatory for compliance audits.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Security team cannot update roles correctly without knowing what authorization objects the enhancement uses. Missing authorization documentation = missing role updates = users get 'no authorization' errors on go-live day = P1 ticket. Documentation prevents this entirely.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Skipping authorization documentation and only providing functional specification. Functional spec tells what the enhancement does — security documentation tells what authorization checks exist and what role changes are needed. Both are required for a complete handover.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Authorization documentation template (as code comment)
"============================================
" ENHANCEMENT: ZCL_MIGO_VENDOR_PRICE_BADI
" TRANSACTION: MIGO
" TRIGGERED BY: MB_MIGO_BADI->POST_DOCUMENT
"--------------------------------------------
" DATA ACCESS:
"   READ:  EKPO (purchase order prices) - CONFIDENTIAL
"   READ:  ZVEND_PRICE_LOG (custom audit log)
"   WRITE: ZVEND_AUDIT (custom write)
"--------------------------------------------
" AUTHORIZATION CHECKS:
"   AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZVENDOR_PRICE'
"     ID BUKRS (company code)
"     ID ACTVT 03 (display)
"   AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT 'ZAUDIT_WRITE'
"     ID ACTVT 01 (create)
"--------------------------------------------
" ROLES TO UPDATE:
"   Z_MM_BUYER: Add ZVENDOR_PRICE BUKRS=* ACTVT=03
"   Z_MM_MANAGER: Add ZVENDOR_PRICE BUKRS=* ACTVT=03,06
"============================================

followupAnswerBank.question: What happens if you forget to transport a new authorization object to production?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Agar authorization object transport nahi hua but enhancement code transport ho gayi — production mein enhancement AUTHORITY-CHECK karta hai ek aisa object ke liye jo exist hi nahi karta. Result depends on SAP behavior: kuch versions mein missing authorization object pe AUTHORITY-CHECK automatically pass ho jaata hai (no object = no restriction) — yeh security hole hai. Kuch configurations mein error aata hai. Aur agar Basis team ne role mein object add kiya hai with wrong field values ya object not activated — also causes issues. Solution: transport sequence — pehle authorization object transport karo (if custom), phir enhancement, phir role changes.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: If a custom authorization object is not transported to production but the enhancement code references it in AUTHORITY-CHECK, the behavior depends on SAP configuration: in some setups, a missing authorization object causes the check to pass without restriction — creating a security hole where no user is checked. In others it may cause a runtime error. The safe transport sequence is: first transport the authorization object definition, then the enhancement implementation, then the role changes that include the new object. Missing object transport is a common go-live issue — include it explicitly in the transport checklist and verify in SUIM or SE11 that the object exists in the target system before go-live.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: This is a real go-live mistake pattern: developer transports enhancement but forgets the custom authorization object. In production, AUTHORITY-CHECK on a non-existent object may silently pass — all users get access. Security audit later finds the gap. Proper transport checklist prevents this.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming that all objects in the transport request are automatically in the right order. Authorization objects must be explicitly added to transport requests and transported before the code that references them. Check SE11 in target system to verify object existence after transport import.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Transport checklist for authorization objects:
" 1. SE11: Verify ZVENDOR_PRICE object created in DEV
" 2. SE09: Check transport request includes:
"    Object type: R3TR SUSO ZVENDOR_PRICE (auth object)
"    Object type: R3TR CLAS ZCL_MIGO_BADI (enhancement class)
"    Object type: R3TR ROLE Z_MM_BUYER (role with new auth)
" 3. Transport SEQUENCE to QA:
"    Transport 1: SUSO ZVENDOR_PRICE (object def first)
"    Transport 2: CLAS ZCL_MIGO_BADI (enhancement second)
"    Transport 3: ROLE Z_MM_BUYER (role last)
" 4. QA verification:
"    SE11: Check ZVENDOR_PRICE exists in QA
"    SU53: Test with QA user -> correct auth behavior
" 5. Same sequence for PRD
